items.title: Multifactorial etiology
items.contentHtml:
Multifactorial etiology: prematurity and ischemia

  • Prematurity is the main risk factor; Immaturity of the intestinal barrier and vascular regulation predisposes to ischemia.
  • Intestinal ischemia is produced by hypoperfusion (shock, asphyxia, patent ductus arteriosus) and activates the inflammatory cascade.
  • Abnormal bacterial colonization and early enteral feeding with formula (not breast milk) act as cofactors in the injury.

items.foco: Classic triad: distension, bleeding, bilious vomiting
items.title: classical triad
items.contentHtml:
Classic triad: distention, bleeding, bilious vomiting

  • The typical presentation includes progressive abdominal distention, rectal bleeding (occult or frank) and bilious vomiting.
  • In very low birth weight premature infants the triad may be incomplete; isolated bloating or feeding intolerance are warning signs.
  • The presence of bloody stools in a premature baby with bloating requires necrotizing enterocolitis to be ruled out before other causes.

items.title: Intestinal pneumatosis on plain x-ray
items.contentHtml:
Intestinal pneumatosis on plain radiograph

  • Intestinal pneumatosis (gas in the intestinal wall) is a pathognomonic finding on plain abdominal radiography.
  • It is observed as linear or cystic images in the intestinal contour, best seen in the supine position or in lateral decubitus projection.
  • The absence of pneumatosis does not rule out necrotizing enterocolitis; The diagnosis is based on clinical symptoms and other radiological findings such as fixed loops or distention.

items.title: Portal-hepatic sign on ultrasound
items.contentHtml:
Hepatic portal sign on ultrasound

  • The hepatic portal sign (gas in the portal system) is a specific ultrasound finding of advanced necrotizing enterocolitis.
  • It is visualized as linear or branched echoes within the liver parenchyma, indicating extension of the gas from the intestinal wall.
  • Its presence is associated with greater severity and risk of perforation; In the modified Bell classification it corresponds to stage III.

items.title: Modified Bell classification
items.contentHtml:
Modified Bell Classification

  • The modified Bell classification stratifies necrotizing enterocolitis into three stages: I (suspected), II (confirmation) and III (advanced).
  • Stage I: nonspecific signs (distention, vomiting) without pneumatosis. Stage II: pneumatosis intestinalis on radiography, with mild systemic signs.
  • Stage III: signs of perforation (pneumoperitoneum), metabolic acidosis, coagulopathy or hypotension; requires urgent surgical management.

items.title: Surgical indication: pneumoperitoneum
items.contentHtml:
Surgical indication: pneumoperitoneum

  • The presence of pneumoperitoneum on plain radiography (free air under the diaphragm) is the main absolute surgical indication.
  • Other relative indications include failure of medical treatment, palpable abdominal mass or signs of peritonitis (wall erythema).
  • The procedure of choice is exploratory laparotomy with intestinal resection and, in selected cases, drainage. peritoneal as a bridge.

items.foco: Very low birth weight premature infant
items.title: Very low birth weight premature infant
items.contentHtml:
Very low birth weight premature infant

  • The very low birth weight premature infant (weight <1500 grams) has the highest risk of developing necrotizing enterocolitis.
  • Immune immaturity, splanchnic hypoperfusion and pathological bacterial colonization explain the high incidence in this group.
  • Prophylaxis with milk Maternal feedingand restriction of early enteral feeding are key preventive measures in these patients.

items.foco: Breast milk as a protective factor
items.title: Breast milk as a protective factor
items.contentHtml:
Breast milk as a protective factor

  • Breast milk significantly reduces the risk of necrotizing enterocolitis in premature infants, with a dose-response effect.
  • It contains immunoglobulins (secretory IgA), prebiotic oligosaccharides and anti-inflammatory factors that protect the intestinal mucosa.
  • When breast milk is not available, hydrolyzed or premature formula is recommended, although with less effect protective than breast milk.

items.title: Medical treatment: fasting and antibiotics
items.contentHtml:
Medical treatment: fasting and antibiotics

  • Initial medical treatment includes absolute fasting (suspending enteral feeding) and decompression with an orogastric tube.
  • Broad-spectrum antibiotics are administered: ampicillin, gentamicin and metronidazole (covering gram-negative and anaerobes).
  • Hemodynamic support is maintained with intravenous fluids and electrolyte correction; The duration of fasting is 7 to 14 days depending on evolution.

items.foco: Use of orogastric tube for decompression
items.title: Use of orogastric tube for decompression
items.contentHtml:
Use of an orogastric tube for decompression

  • The placement of an orogastric tube (not nasogastric, due to the risk of nasal injury in premature babies) allows continuous gastric decompression.
  • It is connected to intermittent aspiration or a gravity bag to evacuate gastric contents and measure waste, evaluating tolerance.
  • The volume and characteristics of the aspirate (bilious or fecaloid) guide the progression of the disease and the need for surgery.

items.title: Intestinal perforation as a complication
items.contentHtml:
Intestinal perforation as a complication

  • Intestinal perforation is the most serious complication of necrotizing enterocolitis, with a mortality of 20-40%.
  • It is manifested by pneumoperitoneum on radiography, sudden clinical deterioration (hypotension, acidosis, plateletopenia) and signs of peritonitis.
  • Treatment is urgent surgery; Isolated perforation of the ileum or right colon is the most common location in premature infants.

items.title: Follow-up with late intestinal stricture
items.contentHtml:
Follow-up with late intestinal stenosis

  • Late intestinal stenosis is a complication that appears weeks or months after the acute episode of necrotizing enterocolitis.
  • It is caused by scarring fibrosis, most common in the left colon or ileum, and manifests with partial intestinal obstruction.
  • The diagnosis is made with an opaque enema or magnetic resonance imaging; The treatment is surgical (resection of the stenotic segment).
